text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic Pringle maneuver by creating a window in the pars flaccida. A disposable plastic trocar is inserted into the left upper abdomen, and umbilical tape is threaded through the window and the foramen of Winslow. The tape, marked at its center, is aligned and its ends are pulled through the trocar, which has soft edges to prevent trauma to the porta hepatis.

During the right hepatectomy, bleeding is observed from a portal branch. The surgeon deploys the Pringle maneuver by advancing the trocar over the umbilical tape and externally tightening a clamp, achieving good hemostasis to allow the resection to proceed.

A trocar and grasper are introduced through a port high in the right upper abdomen. The surgeon navigates the grasper through the foramen of Winslow to grab the marked umbilical tape at its midpoint. The ends of the tape are then brought through the plastic disposable trocar, preparing for the Pringle maneuver if needed.

The surgeon places a Rommel tourniquet laparoscopically by deploying an umbilical tape. The ends of the tape are brought out through a lower abdominal incision and loaded onto a red rubber catheter. A clamp is placed externally, and the red rubber catheter is advanced into position, mimicking the function of a Rommel tourniquet in open surgery.
